bootstrap: Obtain an existing EdgeGrid credential (.edgerc: host, client_token, client_secret, access_token) from your Akamai Control Center account — no self-serve issuance of the first credential.
auth_model: EdgeGrid EG1-HMAC-SHA256 request signing with an existing bootstrap credential; no OAuth, no bearer PAT, no browser.
credential_ttl_rotation: Akamai issues an active credential that expires ~2 years out by default; clientSecret is shown exactly once at creation; response includes expiresOn.
gates: Must already have an Akamai Control Center account and an existing .edgerc credential
new-client apiAccess/groupAccess grant shape varies by account
idempotency: None — default mode always mints a fresh self credential; --new-client always creates a new client (no name-clash read-back).
scope_model: API client access grants: apiAccess (allAccessibleApis or per-API) plus groupAccess (cloned from authorizing user); tighten for least privilege.
verify_notes: maxBody cap (131072 bytes) on hashed bodies, no extra signed headers canonicalized, self-credentials body may need {} on some accounts, and createCredential:true has historically omitted clientSecret (akamai/akamai-apis issue #13).
quirks: Credential is a quad (host/client_token/client_secret/access_token) not an id/secret pair; two modes (self-credential vs new client); new-client responses may omit the secret so --self is the default.

bootstrap: Get a Google OAuth2 access token for an Apigee org you administer, e.g. export GOOGLE_ACCESS_TOKEN="$(gcloud auth print-access-token)" (or mint from a service account with Apigee Developer/Org Admin role).
auth_model: Google OAuth2 bearer access token (short-lived, cloud IAM-gated), Authorization: Bearer on all management calls.
credential_ttl_rotation: --key-expires-in sets key lifetime in ms (-1 = never expire); credential response includes expiresAt.
gates: Google Cloud IAM: caller needs Apigee Developer Admin / Organization Admin role on the org
no browser OAuth self-registration
idempotency: Developer create treats 409 as already-exists; app create on 409 reads the existing app back by name and returns its credentials.
scope_model: API products attached to the developer app (repeatable --api-product); consumerKey grants access per product.
verify_notes: Verify the exact 409 status/wording for duplicate developer apps on your org.
quirks: Two-resource model (Developer entity then Developer App under it); credentials live in a credentials[] array; legacy Apigee Edge uses a different host (api.enterprise.apigee.com) and auth — script targets Apigee X only.

bootstrap: Manually create an OAuth 2.0 integration in the Atlassian Developer Console (no API for app creation), set callback to exactly http://127.0.0.1:8765/callback, and export the client_id/secret.
auth_model: Confidential-client OAuth 2.0 (3LO) authorization-code flow via popped browser + local callback server; JSON-body token exchange with client_secret (no PKCE).
credential_ttl_rotation: Token response includes expires_in; offline_access scope in the default set yields a refresh_token.
gates: App must be created by hand in the Developer Console first
granted scopes must map to a product site or accessible-resources returns empty
app must be added to an Atlassian site
idempotency: N/A — mints no app; each run is a fresh browser login/token exchange for the pre-existing app.
scope_model: Space-separated OAuth scope strings (Jira/Confluence granular scopes), configured on the app in the console and requested via --scope; default "read:jira-user read:me offline_access".
quirks: Requires audience=api.atlassian.com param; per-site cloudid indirection (API calls go to api.atlassian.com/ex/jira/{cloudid}/...); token exchange takes a JSON body not form-encoded; manual API tokens exist as a Basic-auth alternative.

bootstrap: Bring a Management API token (AUTH0_MGMT_TOKEN) or an M2M app's client_id/secret authorized for create:clients/read:clients; or enable Dynamic Client Registration on the tenant for the --dcr path.
auth_model: Bearer Management API token (pasted or minted via client_credentials against /oauth/token); DCR path is tokenless unless tenant runs strict mode.
gates: DCR must be explicitly enabled on the tenant (Dashboard > Settings > Advanced)
DCR requires Professional/Enterprise plan
strict-mode DCR requires an initial access token
Mgmt token needs create:clients (and read:clients) scopes
idempotency: Mgmt path: 409 name clash falls back to a Lucene search (GET /api/v2/clients?q=name:"...") and returns the existing client.
scope_model: OAuth grant_types + token_endpoint_auth_method on the client; Mgmt token itself gated by scope strings (create:clients, read:clients).
verify_notes: DCR is documented to return 201 per RFC 7591 but Auth0 has been observed returning 200/201; strict-mode tenants require an initial access token.
quirks: Two front doors auto-selected: RFC 7591 DCR (POST /oidc/register, off by default and plan-gated) vs Management API default path; description truncated to 140 chars for the Mgmt API.

bootstrap: Have IAM credentials (access key id/secret, optionally STS session token) for the AWS account that owns the API, plus an existing usage plan id.
auth_model: AWS SigV4 (AWS4-HMAC-SHA256) request signing with the caller's own IAM credentials; hand-rolled signer, no aws-sdk, no browser/OAuth.
gates: No public app-registration/OAuth flow — must be inside the AWS account
key only works against methods with apiKeyRequired=true
key must be attached to a usage plan
idempotency: CreateApiKey 409 (ConflictException) triggers GET /apikeys?nameQuery=...&includeValues=true to recover the existing key by exact name; usage-plan attach 409 treated as already-attached success.
scope_model: Usage plans (throttle/quota tiers) — the key is attached to a plan rather than granted scopes.
verify_notes: Verify the SigV4 signed-header set, the platform URI encoder for reserved chars, and that includeValues=true is needed to read key values back (nameQuery is a prefix match).
quirks: Regional control-plane host (apigateway.{region}.amazonaws.com); credential is a downstream api-key header value, not an OAuth pair; two-step create-then-attach-to-usage-plan.

bootstrap: Have IAM credentials for the AWS account and a Cognito user pool id you control (COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID).
auth_model: AWS SigV4 signing (service cognito-idp) over the AWS JSON 1.1 RPC protocol — single POST to / with X-Amz-Target header; no browser, no OAuth/PKCE.
gates: No anonymous DCR endpoint and no browser-OAuth self-serve path — credentials come from the pool-owning AWS account
idempotency: Cognito does NOT enforce unique ClientName (no 409), so the script lists clients first (paginated ListUserPoolClients), matches exactly by name, and only creates when none is found; DescribeUserPoolClient recovers the secret.
scope_model: ExplicitAuthFlows list on the app client (defaults ALLOW_REFRESH_TOKEN_AUTH + ALLOW_USER_SRP_AUTH); confidential vs public toggled by GenerateSecret.
quirks: RPC-style API (all calls POST /, action in X-Amz-Target, content-type application/x-amz-json-1.1); duplicate names allowed so idempotency is list-first not 409-driven; secret only generated when --secret is passed (default public client).

bootstrap: Get an ARM bearer token as an Azure RBAC principal with write access, e.g. az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com; also know the subscription/resource-group/service/product ids.
auth_model: Azure AD bearer token for the ARM management plane (audience https://management.azure.com/), supplied out-of-band via env var.
gates: No third-party self-serve registration — must be an authenticated Azure RBAC principal (API Management Service Contributor or write access)
idempotency: Idempotent PUT: 201 = newly created, 200 = subscription already exists (keys read back either way); user creation is also an idempotent PUT.
scope_model: APIM products — the subscription is scoped to /products/{productId} (tiers like "starter", "unlimited").
quirks: The "app" is a subscription, not an OAuth client; runtime credential is the Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header; keys are blank on PUT/GET and must be read via a separate POST /listSecrets; two-key (primary/secondary) rotation design; optional owner-user provisioning.

bootstrap: Mint a bootstrap API Token in the dashboard (My Profile > API Tokens > Create Token) with the "API Tokens Write" / Create Additional Tokens permission and export it.
auth_model: Bearer API Token on every call (Authorization: Bearer); no DCR, no OAuth/PKCE browser dance.
credential_ttl_rotation: Token value is shown ONCE by Cloudflare; optional --expires-on RFC3339 expiry; existing tokens can only be rolled, not reprinted.
gates: Bootstrap token must hold API Tokens Write permission
bootstrap token must verify as active before minting
idempotency: User tokens: lists /user/tokens and if a same-named token exists reports its id (secret cannot be reprinted) instead of duplicating; account-scoped tokens skip the dup-check.
scope_model: Permission groups (matched by name, e.g. "DNS Read") combined into allow/deny policies over resource scopes (account-level or wildcard); browsable via --list-permissions.
verify_notes: Permission-group list only fetches the default page (supports ?name=/?scope= filters), and account-scoped token listing is not clearly documented so dup-check only runs for user tokens.
quirks: Tokens-minting-tokens model (child token from bootstrap token); single opaque secret (no id/secret pair); two owner types — user tokens (/user/tokens) vs account tokens (/accounts/{id}/tokens) selected by env var; envelope response { success, errors, result }.

bootstrap: Create the application by hand in the Discord Developer Portal (https://discord.com/developers/applications) and copy the Client ID and Client Secret from the OAuth2 tab.
auth_model: OAuth2 client_credentials grant with HTTP Basic auth (client_id:client_secret); no browser, no user, no PKCE — app-only bearer token.
credential_ttl_rotation: The client_credentials token is short-lived (expires_in printed); the client secret can be reset in the portal.
gates: No public API to create an application or mint credentials — Developer Portal only
idempotency: N/A — creates nothing; it only exercises and verifies credentials that already exist.
scope_model: Space-separated OAuth2 scope strings (default "identify"); client_credentials tokens are broadly granted identify + applications.commands.update.
verify_notes: GET /oauth2/applications/@me may return 401/403 under some scope configurations; script degrades to the application object embedded in /oauth2/@me.
quirks: Verification-only script (proves credentials work rather than minting any); sends a custom User-Agent; token endpoint rejects JSON bodies (form-urlencoded only).

bootstrap: None — no token or env var needed; just a browser and a GitHub login to approve the app creation (name must be globally unique).
auth_model: Browser-based GitHub App Manifest flow: local server serves an auto-POSTing manifest form to github.com/settings/apps/new, catches the redirect code, then exchanges it UNauthenticated at the conversions endpoint.
credential_ttl_rotation: The one-time manifest code is valid for one hour; all credentials returned in a single 201 response.
gates: GitHub App name must be globally unique
user must approve creation in the browser
--org requires permission on that organization
idempotency: None — each run registers a brand-new app; a name collision fails at GitHub's form.
scope_model: Manifest-declared default_permissions map + default_events (script sends empty/none, private app, webhooks inactive).
quirks: Manifest must be a form-POST body field (not a GET query param), so the script serves a local auto-submitting HTML form; conversion exchange is unauthenticated (the code IS the credential); returns five credentials at once including a PEM private key; personal vs org endpoints.

bootstrap: Platform team must stand up a Gloo Portal (PortalConfig + ext-auth + OIDC IdP); you then obtain an OIDC bearer access token (client-credentials from the IdP, or copied from a portal session) into GLOO_TOKEN.
auth_model: OIDC bearer access token (Authorization: Bearer) against the Portal Server REST API; token obtained out-of-band from the portal's IdP.
credential_ttl_rotation: Key value returned exactly once at creation and cannot be retrieved again — only rotated by creating a new key.
gates: No public DCR — portal must be stood up by the platform team
usage-plan visibility/subscription is controlled by the platform team via PortalConfig and ext-auth
token audience must match the Portal Server
idempotency: Lists existing keys (GET /api-keys) and if a same-named key exists under the plan, reports it (secret not re-revealed) instead of creating a duplicate.
scope_model: Usage plans — the API key is scoped to a usage plan that maps to API products; no client_secret concept.
verify_notes: Base-path mount varies by install (/v1 vs /portal-server/v1) and the script warns if the URL lacks /v1; list-response shapes are defensively parsed.
quirks: Kubernetes/Envoy-native — the "app" concept is a usage-plan subscription; the Portal Server REST API is distinct from both the developer-portal web UI and the Envoy data plane (404 hints at pointing to the wrong one).

bootstrap: GOOGLE_ACCESS_TOKEN=$(gcloud auth print-access-token) from an identity with iam.serviceAccounts.create + iam.serviceAccountKeys.create (serviceAccountAdmin + serviceAccountKeyAdmin or Owner) on the project.
auth_model: Google OAuth2 bearer access token (cloud IAM) against the IAM REST API; no browser flow in the script.
credential_ttl_rotation: Key creation is the one-and-only chance to read the private key (Google keeps only the public half); max 10 user-managed keys per service account (RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED).
gates: IAM permissions/roles required on the project
org policy constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation can block key creation
IAM API must be enabled
OAuth client IDs remain console-only
idempotency: 409 ALREADY_EXISTS on service-account create triggers a GET read-back and reuse, then a fresh key is minted for the existing account.
quirks: Honest substitution: Google OAuth client_id/client_secret pairs have NO creation API (Cloud Console only), so the script mints the agent-appropriate credential instead — a service account + base64-decoded private-key JSON; SA id has a strict 6-30 char regex.

bootstrap: Generate a Personal Access Token once in the APIM Console (user profile -> Tokens -> "Generate a personal token") and paste it via GRAVITEE_TOKEN; know your Management API base URL.
auth_model: Personal Access Token sent as Authorization: Bearer against the self-hosted Management API; no browser dance.
gates: Subscription API keys may be pending approval ("no API key yet") depending on plan settings
self-hosted — you need access to a deployed APIM instance
idempotency: Create returning 400/409 (duplicate name on some deployments) falls back to listing the caller's applications and reusing the exact-name match.
scope_model: API plans — the app subscribes to a plan on an API; the script auto-picks the first API_KEY-security plan unless --plan is given.
verify_notes: Plan-listing shape varies across APIM versions (documented v2 path used); pass --plan explicitly if your version differs.
quirks: Mixes mAPI v1 (application create/list) and v2 (plans/subscriptions/keys) URL prefixes; org/environment path segments default to DEFAULT; the sibling Gravitee Access Management product does offer true RFC 7591 DCR — a separate product.

bootstrap: Create a public app in your HubSpot Developer Account (Apps -> Create app), register http://127.0.0.1:8765/callback as a redirect URL, export client_id/secret — or create a private app and set HUBSPOT_TOKEN to its pat- token.
auth_model: Confidential OAuth 2.0 authorization-code grant via browser + local callback (client_id+client_secret; HubSpot does NOT support PKCE), OR a private-app pat- token used as Bearer (skips OAuth).
credential_ttl_rotation: OAuth path prints expires_in and a refresh_token for rotation.
gates: No public API to create an application — dashboard-only app creation
requested scopes must be a subset of the scopes configured on the app
idempotency: N/A — creates nothing; each run is a fresh OAuth login or PAT verification.
scope_model: Space-separated OAuth scope strings configured on the app (default "oauth"); private apps carry their own scope set.
verify_notes: account-info is used to validate private-app (pat-) tokens because the access-tokens/{token} endpoint is not documented to accept them.
quirks: Two app species with different verify endpoints: public apps (developer account, OAuth, no PKCE) vs private apps (portal-scoped pat-na1/pat-eu1 regional tokens); HUBSPOT_TOKEN presence switches mode entirely.

bootstrap: Bring a per-catalog portal OIDC bearer token (APIC_TOKEN) — via the portal password grant using a consumer toolkit app's client_id/secret, or copied from a logged-in portal browser session — plus provider org/catalog/consumer org names.
auth_model: Bearer token minted by the catalog's portal OIDC IdP, plus a mandatory X-IBM-Consumer-Context header (providerOrg.catalog) on every call; optional in-script password-grant token bootstrap.
credential_ttl_rotation: client_secret is only returned at creation time; reused apps may show a blank secret — rotate the credential in the portal for a fresh one.
gates: No global vendor-wide sign-in — every call scoped to one provider org + catalog
need a consumer-org membership and a portal identity
toolkit app credentials needed for the password-grant bootstrap
idempotency: 409 or already-exists/duplicate error text triggers a list-apps read-back by name and reuse (secret unavailable on reuse).
scope_model: Product plans — optional --product/--plan subscribes the app to a published product's plan.
verify_notes: Verify the credential container name against your catalog's consumer-api version — creation returns app_credentials[] but older builds nest it under credentials.
quirks: Three-axis scoping (provider org + catalog + consumer org) with the odd X-IBM-Consumer-Context routing header (missing it yields 404/401); realm string format consumer:{porg}:{catalog}/{catalog}-idp; SaaS vs self-managed base hosts differ.

bootstrap: Create one M2M application in the Kinde dashboard, authorize it for the Kinde Management API with create:applications + read:applications scopes, and export its client_id/secret (or paste a pre-obtained KINDE_TOKEN).
auth_model: OAuth2 client_credentials grant (audience {domain}/api — NOT /api/v1) minting a Management API bearer token, or a pasted management bearer token.
gates: No RFC 7591 DCR
M2M app must be explicitly authorized for the Management API with create:applications and read:applications scopes
idempotency: Opt-in only: --reuse does a paginated exact-name list lookup then GET-by-id to recover the secret; without --reuse Kinde happily creates duplicate names (no 409).
scope_model: Application type enum (reg | spa | m2m | device, default m2m); Management API access gated by scope strings on the M2M app.
verify_notes: Kinde does not appear to reject duplicate application names at create time — verify against your tenant; list items omit the secret so a follow-up GET-by-id is required.
quirks: Audience gotcha: token audience is {domain}/api not {domain}/api/v1; domain normalizer accepts bare subdomain, subdomain.kinde.com, or full URL; GET application returns the client_secret (retrievable, unlike most providers).

bootstrap: For registration: have a Dev Portal URL plus a developer portalaccesstoken from a logged-in session (or developer email/password for the script to log in); for the admin --list path: a kpat_ Personal Access Token from Konnect.
auth_model: Two token planes: kpat_ PAT as Bearer against the Konnect platform API (admin --list), and a developer portalaccesstoken sent as BOTH Bearer header and cookie against the portal's own /api (optionally minted via basic username/password login).
gates: Registration may require portal-admin approval before credentials appear
auth_strategy_id required when a portal exposes more than one strategy
PAT cannot create apps or read secrets on a developer's behalf — apps are developer-owned
OIDC secrets may be shown once in the UI only
idempotency: 409 on application create triggers a GET list of the developer's applications and exact-name read-back.
scope_model: Per-API auth strategies (Key-Auth vs OIDC/DCR) chosen by the portal; credential shape depends on the published API's strategy.
verify_notes: Per-geo host spelling, developer-login path/version (/api/v3 vs /api/v2), applications path variant, create-body fields, and the registration-vs-credential sub-resource split all vary by portal.
quirks: Two front doors — admin platform API (regional hosts us|eu|au|me|in|sg + global, kpat_ PAT) vs Dev Portal developer API on the portal's own host with a cookie-based portalaccesstoken (sent as both header and cookie); credential type depends on the API's auth strategy.

bootstrap: Obtain a Microsoft Graph bearer token with Application.ReadWrite.OwnedBy (or .All) / Application Developer role, e.g. az account get-access-token --resource https://graph.microsoft.com.
auth_model: Microsoft Graph bearer access token supplied via env var (delegated or client_credentials against login.microsoftonline.com); no DCR, no browser self-serve.
credential_ttl_rotation: client_secret (secretText) is shown ONCE and is unrecoverable; response includes keyId and endDateTime expiry.
gates: Token needs Application.ReadWrite.OwnedBy or Application.ReadWrite.All permission (or Application Developer role / member user)
idempotency: Pre-create $filter lookup by exact displayName; if found, reuses the app and mints a fresh secret (heuristic — Entra allows duplicate displayNames, first hit wins).
scope_model: signInAudience option (AzureADMyOrg / AzureADMultipleOrgs / AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount) plus Graph application permissions gating the caller; no API-product concept.
quirks: Three-step chain (application -> addPassword -> servicePrincipal) with two distinct ids (object id vs appId/client_id); service-principal creation is a separate resource that 409s if already provisioned and is skippable via --no-service-principal; duplicate displayNames are legal so name lookup is heuristic.
